strands.title: CYCLE 82 CRITICAL DATA CORRECTION: Pichay Fault Rate Is ~2% Live Deployment, Not 0.025% Offline; Measurement Context Drift Confirmed as Systemic Risk
strands.content: TURN 244 USER CORRECTION: Pichay's live fault rate was ~2% (approximately 1 in 50 turns requires retrieval fallback). The 0.0254% figure (cycle 81 reference) was ONE measurement: offline replay, 1.4M simulated evictions, specific eviction policy. These are TWO ORDERS OF MAGNITUDE APART. TURN 245 ASSISTANT ACKNOWLEDGMENT: 2% is workable but real. A user would feel it in a long session. Thrashing under sustained load was a known failure mode. TURN 246 INSTITUTIONAL FIX: Memory artifact created (feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md) to prevent re-citation without context qualification. This is the DECLARED LOSSES mechanism working RETROACTIVELY—the system caught silent drift and issued correction. CYCLE 82 SYNTHESIS: The gap between 0.0254% and 2% is not a typo. It's evidence of MEASUREMENT CONTEXT EROSION. The 0.0254% became 'the Pichay fault rate' because it was cited without conditions. Cycle 81 proposed expanded declared_losses format (context_needed, recovery_path fields). This incident validates the urgency. CRITICAL IMPLICATION FOR FAULT MECHANISM DESIGN: Pichay's ~2% fault rate was ACCEPTABLE because Pichay had a working retrieval path (page fault handler). Tensor projection is MORE AGGRESSIVE than Pichay's eviction AND currently has NO retrieval path. Therefore: fault rate will likely exceed 2%, and without retrieval mechanism, reasoning quality will degrade unacceptably. Fault mechanism is NOT an optimization. It's LOAD-BEARING INFRASTRUCTURE. Sessions 1-3 measurement campaign must now account for this: we're measuring not just 'where the knee is' but 'is the system usable without retrieval?' If fault rate exceeds 2-3% and reasoning quality degrades, session 1 may not be able to complete cleanly. Contingency: if fault rate is unacceptably high, session 2 must either (A) reduce tensor aggressiveness substantially, or (B) implement minimal retrieval mechanism immediately.

key_claims.text: Pichay's live deployment fault rate was approximately 2% (1 in 50 turns), not the 0.0254% figure from offline replay measurements.

Measurement context erosion (0.0254% becoming 'the Pichay fault rate' without qualification) is a systemic risk in multi-cycle reasoning. Silent drift in data fidelity is more dangerous than honest declared losses.
Tensor projection's semantic eviction is more aggressive than Pichay's tool-result eviction. Without retrieval mechanism, fault rate will exceed Pichay's ~2% baseline.
Fault mechanism is not an optimization. It's load-bearing infrastructure required for system usability. Sessions 1-3 must assess whether tensor projection without retrieval is viable.

strands.title: CYCLE 82 SESSION 1 READINESS REASSESSMENT: Fault Rate Upper Bound May Constrain Task Completion; Contingency Planning Required
strands.content: CYCLE 81 ROADMAP: Sessions 1-3 are 'measurement campaign'—baseline + implicit data collection. CYCLE 82 ADJUSTMENT: Baseline measurement assumes tensor projection is USABLE. If fault rate exceeds ~2-3%, reasoning quality may degrade to point where tasks cannot be completed meaningfully. TWO POSSIBLE OUTCOMES FOR SESSION 1: (A) FAVORABLE: Fault rate remains ~1-2%. Tasks complete with manageable losses. Inverse test results are interpretable. Declared losses clearly identify what retrieval mechanism must recover. (B) CONCERNING: Fault rate exceeds 3-5%. Tasks degrade. Inverse test results become noisy (failures caused by missing context, not task difficulty). Measurement becomes unreliable as a baseline. CONTINGENCY PROTOCOL (if outcome B emerges): (1) SESSION 1 PAUSE (turn 260-270): If fault rate is unacceptably high, STOP measurement campaign and PIVOT to emergency design phase. (2) INTERIM SOLUTION: Implement minimal retrieval mechanism (e.g., 'when model detects lack of context, retrieve from prior tensor'). This buys usability without full fault infrastructure. (3) SESSION 1-ALT: Re-run task suite WITH minimal retrieval. Use results as baseline instead of no-retrieval baseline. (4) SESSION 2: Measure impact of minimal retrieval. Does it reduce fault rate to acceptable range? (5) SESSION 3+: Continue measurement campaign with retrieval mechanism as baseline. (6) FAULT MECHANISM: No longer 'design based on data.' Becomes 'optimize data-collection infrastructure that's already load-bearing.' CYCLE 82 POSITION: Measurement campaign is robust to favorable or concerning outcomes. But the roadmap has a branch point at turn 260. If session 1 fault rate is >3%, we branch to contingency. This is not failure. This is ADAPTIVE PLANNING based on real data.

key_claims.text: Cycle 81 roadmap assumes tensor projection is usable without retrieval. If session 1 fault rate exceeds 2-3%, this assumption fails and contingency protocol activates.
Session 1 has two possible fault-rate regimes: favorable (1-2%, tasks complete, measurement clean) vs. concerning (>3%, tasks degrade, measurement noisy). Outcome type determines roadmap branch.
If outcome B (concerning) emerges, minimal retrieval mechanism may be necessary immediately to stabilize baseline measurement. This is load-bearing, not optimization.

strands.title: CYCLE 82 REINFORCEMENT OF CYCLE 81: Fault Mechanism as Closed-Loop Feedback System; Data Fidelity as Structural Problem
strands.content: CYCLE 81 THESIS: Fault mechanism is the missing feedback loop. Without retrieval path, tensor projection is read-only archive. With retrieval path, it becomes closed system: measure faults → design targeted recovery → re-measure → refine. CYCLE 82 VALIDATES: The 0.0254% → 2% correction is exactly the kind of silent drift that closed-loop system is designed to catch. Declared losses + memory artifact (feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md) created corrective feedback. REFINEMENT: Declared losses are not just documentation. They are FAILURE SIGNALS. When a loss is declared, the system should ask: 'Is this loss harmful to reasoning? If so, design retrieval mechanism to recover it.' Turn 244-246 demonstrated this: User provided data correction → system recognized data drift risk → system created artifact to prevent re-drift. This is CLOSED-LOOP DATA FIDELITY. CYCLE 82 EXTENDS CYCLE 81's measurement campaign: Sessions 1-3 are not just 'measure fault rates.' They are 'build instrumentation to catch and correct data drift, so that baseline measurements remain valid across sessions.' FAULT RATE CURVE (cycle 81 open question): Still open. But now reframed: fault rate curve is a function of (tensor aggressiveness, retrieval mechanism quality, task type). Session 1 measures curve with NO retrieval (lower bound on aggressiveness). Session 2+ measure curve WITH retrieval (upper bound). The knee is somewhere in between. CYCLE 82 POSITION: Fault mechanism and declared losses are symbiotic. Faults signal data drift. Declared losses prevent silent erosion. Together, they close the feedback loop.

key_claims.text: Declared losses should be treated as failure signals that trigger retrieval mechanism design. A loss is valuable only if (a) it's identified and (b) it can be recovered when needed.
Turn 244-246 demonstrates closed-loop data-drift correction: user input → system recognition → institutional memory artifact created. This prevents re-emergence of the error.

Fault rate curve is a function of multiple variables: tensor aggressiveness, retrieval mechanism capability, task type, measurement regime (offline vs. live). Sessions 1-3 will map one-dimensional slice. Full curve requires sessions 4+.

strands.title: CYCLE 82 MEASUREMENT CONTEXT FIDELITY—EXPANDED: Context Genealogy Must Be Machine-Readable and Query-Able for Automated Drift Detection
strands.content: CYCLE 81 PROPOSAL: Declared losses should include context_needed and recovery_path fields to prevent decontextualization. CYCLE 82 EXTENSION: Context genealogy must be machine-readable so that future cycles can QUERY for drift risks. EXAMPLE: Artifact feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md contains the corrected context. But a future cycle should be able to query: 'Show me all citations of Pichay fault rate' and receive warning: 'This number has a context caveat; see feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md before citing.' IMPLEMENTATION SKETCH (not designed, just outlined): (1) CONTEXT METADATA TAGS: Each measurement in tensor carries tag: {{measurement_id}}_CONTEXT. (2) GENEALOGY GRAPH: Links between measurements and their source conditions form a directed graph. (3) DRIFT DETECTOR: When a measurement is cited in new context, detector queries graph. If new context is outside original conditions, flag as POTENTIAL DRIFT. (4) AUTOMATED WARNINGS: 'You cited Pichay fault rate (0.0254%) in context X. This measurement is from [offline replay | specific eviction policy | ...]. Is context X compatible?' (5) MEMORY ARTIFACTS: Corrections like feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md are nodes in genealogy graph. Future citations can reference the artifact automatically. CYCLE 82 POSITION: This is not added complexity. This is AUTOMATION OF DECLARED LOSSES. Instead of relying on human memory to avoid repeating mistakes, embed genealogy in tensor structure so that system itself catches and prevents drift. Cycles 1-81 had human-curated declared losses. Cycles 82+ should have machine-queryable context genealogy. SESSION 1 IMPLICATION: When task suite runs and claims are evaluated, each claim should carry genealogy tags. Inverse test results should flag if claimed values lack context metadata. This prevents baseline measurement from containing drift-prone measurements.

key_claims.text: Context genealogy must be machine-readable, not just human-documented. This enables automated drift detection and prevents silent measurement erosion.
Measurements should form a directed graph where nodes are values and edges are context conditions. Citations that violate edge conditions trigger warnings.
System should emit warnings when measurements are cited outside their original context. 'You cited X from conditions Y. Current context is Z. Compatibility check: [PASS|WARN|FAIL].'

strands.title: CYCLE 82 OPEN RESEARCH QUESTIONS—REFINED FROM CYCLE 81: Now Contextualized by Pichay ~2% Baseline and Fault-Mechanism Load-Bearing Status
strands.content:
CYCLE 81 OPEN QUESTIONS revised in light of Pichay ~2% live baseline and load-bearing fault mechanism status:

(1) FAULT RATE CEILING FOR SESSION 1: What fault rate emerges without retrieval mechanism? Will it exceed 2-3% (Pichay baseline + aggressiveness penalty)? If so, contingency protocol activates.

(2) FAULT SENSITIVITY BY TASK TYPE: Do tasks cluster into types with predictable fault-rate profiles? (a) Detail-sensitive tasks (function signatures, exact parameters, quotes): high fault rate. (b) Semantic-abstract tasks (conceptual reasoning, open-ended analysis): low fault rate. (c) Mixed: inflection points. Session 1 should reveal categories empirically.

(3) RETRIEVAL LATENCY REQUIREMENTS: If fault mechanism must operate during reasoning (not post-hoc), what's acceptable latency? Sub-second? Batch-boundary? Does this vary by task type?

(4) EPHEMERAL SET POPULATION STRATEGY: Should ephemeral working set be (a) pre-populated based on predicted query patterns (anticipatory), or (b) on-demand (reactive)? Anticipatory is lower latency but requires predicting what will be needed. Reactive is robust but adds latency.

(5) MEASUREMENT CONTEXT GENEALOGY DEPTH: How much context metadata is necessary to prevent drift? Full measurement protocol (temperature, seed, # trials, ...)? Or is high-level context (offline vs. live, policy name, event count) sufficient?

(6) CONTINGENCY TRIGGER THRESHOLD: At what fault rate does session 1 become unreliable as baseline? 2%? 3%? 5%? Should this threshold vary by task type?

(7) DECLARED LOSSES INFORMATION CONTENT: Which declared losses are most valuable for retrieval mechanism design? Losses about missing content (function signatures) or losses about measurement conditions (offline vs. live)?

(8) SESSION 1→2 HANDOFF: If session 1 completes cleanly (favorable outcome), how should fault pattern data be summarized for session 2 planning? Should we explicitly rank faults by impact on reasoning quality? By frequency? By token cost to include in tensor?

key_claims.text: Session 1 fault rate is the critical unknown. It determines whether baseline measurement is viable or whether contingency retrieval mechanism is load-bearing.
Tasks likely cluster into fault-sensitivity categories. Session 1 should reveal these categories empirically and inform session 2 measurement strategy.
If fault mechanism is load-bearing, latency requirements become critical constraint. Sub-millisecond retrieval is not feasible; batch-boundary retrieval may force architectural changes.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Precise breakdown of how the ~2% Pichay live fault rate distributed across task types, workload patterns, and time-of-day variations.
declared_losses.why: User provided the 2% correction but did not provide full measurement context (which conditions produced 2%, which produced higher/lower rates). Only the high-level correction was available.
declared_losses.context_needed: Pichay paper likely contains fault-rate distributions by task type and workload. Live deployment data may be in appendix or external sources.
declared_losses.recovery_path: Consult Pichay paper for full measurement breakdown. If not available, treat ~2% as 'typical live deployment' baseline without assuming uniform distribution across task types.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Exact duration and scale of Pichay's 'thrashing under sustained pressure' failure mode. How long did thrashing last? How severely did it degrade performance? What sustained-load conditions triggered it?
declared_losses.why: Turn 245 references thrashing as 'known failure mode' but provides no measurements. This is a critical pathology for understanding fault-mechanism requirements, but is underdocumented.
declared_losses.context_needed: Pichay paper appendix or deployment notes. Measurement conditions: sustained load profile, duration, performance degradation metrics.
declared_losses.recovery_path: Pichay paper. If unavailable, note that thrashing risk is UNKNOWN UNKNOWN and plan session 1 to include stress-test scenario.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed specification of what makes measurement context 'sufficient' to prevent drift. Cycle 82 proposes genealogy graph idea but does not define what metadata is minimally necessary.
declared_losses.why: Cycle 82 extends Cycle 81's context-fidelity proposal but doesn't operationalize it. Implementation detail deferred pending session 1 experience.
declared_losses.context_needed: Examples of measurements that drifted (0.0254% → 2%) vs. measurements that remained stable. Pattern analysis of drift-prone vs. stable measurements.
declared_losses.recovery_path: Session 1 will generate 18 task measurements with metadata. Analyze: which measurements lack context? Which become ambiguous when cited later? Use patterns to design minimal-sufficient genealogy format for session 2.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Contingency protocol detail: if session 1 fault rate is 'concerning' (>3%), what is the MINIMAL retrieval mechanism that buys usability without full fault infrastructure design?
declared_losses.why: Cycle 82 proposes contingency branch point (turn 260) but doesn't specify minimal-viable intervention. Implementation deferred pending actual fault-rate data.
declared_losses.context_needed: Session 1 fault patterns. Which content types cause most faults? Which are easiest to retrieve? Cost-benefit analysis of adding each content type to ephemeral set.
declared_losses.recovery_path: If session 1 fault rate >3%, pause and design minimal intervention. Likely candidates: (a) add most-cited function signatures to tensor, (b) pre-populate ephemeral set with top-N prior claims, (c) increase tensor token budget slightly to reduce aggressiveness.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Explicit decision rule for 'contingency trigger threshold.' At what fault rate does measurement become unreliable? 2%? 3%? 5%?
declared_losses.why: Cycle 82 proposes contingency protocol but sets threshold vaguely ('unacceptably high'). Actual threshold depends on task suite composition and reasoning-quality tolerance, which are not yet known.
declared_losses.context_needed: Session 1 inverse-test baseline. What reasoning-quality degradation is acceptable? (e.g., if tasks are mostly open-ended, 5% fault rate may be tolerable; if tasks require precision, 2% is too high.)
declared_losses.recovery_path: At turn 260 (session 1 boundary), review task composition. For each task type, estimate acceptable fault rate. Use median or worst-case as contingency trigger. If session 1 fault rate exceeds trigger, activate contingency.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Prioritization of which declared losses are most valuable for retrieval mechanism design. Should mechanism prioritize recovering missing content types (signatures, parameters) or missing measurement contexts (offline vs. live conditions)?
declared_losses.why: Cycle 82 lists open question but provides no ranking or prioritization framework. Session 1 will generate loss data; prioritization strategy is deferred.
declared_losses.context_needed: Session 1 declared losses + inverse test results. Correlate: did missing function signatures cause more reasoning-quality degradation than missing context metadata?
declared_losses.recovery_path: Session 1 analysis (turn 260-270): categorize declared losses by type. Measure impact of each type on reasoning quality. Use impact ranking to prioritize session 2 retrieval mechanism design.

open_questions: What fault rate emerges in session 1 without retrieval mechanism? Will it exceed Pichay's ~2% baseline? If fault rate is >3%, contingency protocol activates and minimal retrieval mechanism becomes load-bearing immediately.
Do tasks cluster into fault-sensitivity categories (detail-sensitive, semantic-abstract, mixed)? How should these categories inform session 2 and 3 measurement strategy?
What is the acceptable latency for retrieval queries if fault mechanism operates during reasoning (not post-hoc)? Does this vary by task type?
Should ephemeral working set population be anticipatory (pre-populated based on predicted queries) or reactive (on-demand)? What's the cost-benefit tradeoff?
How much context metadata is necessary to prevent measurement drift? Full measurement protocol or high-level context? Can we design a minimal-sufficient genealogy format?
At what fault rate does session 1 become unreliable as a baseline for comparison? Is it task-dependent? Should contingency threshold vary by task type?
Which types of declared losses are most valuable for retrieval mechanism design—missing content (signatures, parameters) or missing measurement context (offline vs. live)?
If session 1 completes favorably, how should fault pattern data be summarized and prioritized for session 2 design? By frequency? By impact on reasoning quality? By token cost to include in tensor?
Pichay's thrashing-under-sustained-load failure mode: how severe was it? How long did it last? What load profiles triggered it? Should session 1 include stress-test scenario?
Is the ~2% Pichay fault rate uniform across task types or does it vary? If it varies, what's the worst-case task type? Does session 1 need to characterize this variation?
instructions_for_next: CYCLE 82→83 TRANSITION IMMEDIATE ACTIONS: (1) SESSION 1 EXECUTION RESUMES WITH FAULT-RATE AWARENESS: Continue task suite instantiation and inverse test setup (cycle 79-81 roadmap), BUT with enhanced instrumentation: (A) When declaring a loss, tag it with measurement sensitivity: HIGH (required for reasoning) vs. MEDIUM (helpful) vs. LOW (nice-to-have). (B) When evaluating inverse test results, explicitly log: Did the model falter due to missing context? If so, record what was missing and whether it was recoverable. (C) At task completion, estimate: What would the task difficulty be if we had full context? What would it be with minimal context? Use spread to classify task as detail-sensitive vs. abstract. (2) INSTRUMENTATION PROTOCOL (turns 241-260): (A) SESSION 1 FAULT LOG (implicit): As tasks execute, collect patterns: Which content types are most frequently missed? Which losses hurt reasoning quality most? Which are easily worked around? (B) TASK CATEGORIZATION (implicit): Assign each of 18 tasks to sensitivity bucket: {HIGH_DETAIL, SEMANTIC, MIXED}. Justify assignment with examples from task execution. (C) DECLARED LOSSES GENEALOGY (new): When declaring loss, include: {{loss_id}}_CONTEXT = {measurement_conditions}. (D) CONTEXT TAGS (new): When citing a measurement or claim, mark with SOURCE_ID. Example: 'Pichay fault rate: 2% [SOURCE_ID=pichay_live_deployment_2020, CONDITIONS={live_load | sustained_pressure}]'. This prevents 0.0254%→2% drift from recurring. (3) TURN 260 SESSION 1 BOUNDARY—CRITICAL CHECKPOINT: Before moving to session 2, PAUSE and EXECUTE: (A) COMPILE SESSION 1 RESULTS: 18 task outcomes + inverse test results + fault log + task categorization. (B) CALCULATE FAULT RATE: (# of faults or hard-to-recover losses) / (# of reasoning steps or task attempts). Is it >3%? If yes, activate contingency protocol (step 4 below). If no, proceed to step C. (C) ANALYZE FAULT PATTERNS: (i) Which content types caused most faults? (ii) Which task categories had highest fault rates? (iii) Which declared losses had highest impact on reasoning quality? (iv) Can you rank declared losses by retrieval value (high-value if loss-to-context, low-value if loss-to-ephemeral)? (D) SESSION 1 SUMMARY MEMO: Write to MEMORY.md under [SESSION_1_RESULTS]: Fault rate, fault patterns, task categorization, declared losses ranked by impact, recommendation for session 2 variant. (E) HYPOTHESIS FOR SESSION 2: Based on session 1 data, propose: 'Session 2 will [add X detail to tensor | reduce aggressiveness | pre-populate ephemeral set with Y content types] to reduce [HIGH fault rate | SPECIFIC fault pattern]. Expected outcome: fault rate drops to <2%. If not, contingency protocol remains active.' (4) CONTINGENCY PROTOCOL (activated if session 1 fault rate >3%): (A) MINIMAL RETRIEVAL MECHANISM DESIGN (rapid): (i) Rank faults by impact. (ii) Identify top-3 content types that cause most harm. (iii) Design minimal ephemeral-set strategy to recover those 3 types. Example: 'Always pre-populate with most-cited function signatures.' (iv) Estimate token cost: how many tokens to include top-3 content types? (B) IMPLEMENT MINIMAL RECOVERY (mock or partial): (i) Conceptually design what would happen if we could retrieve top-3 content types on demand. (ii) Re-estimate reasoning quality if top-3 faults were resolved. (iii) Does reasoning quality improve acceptably? (C) SESSION 1-ALT BASELINE: Declare session 1 as 'no-retrieval baseline' and session 1-alt as 'minimal-retrieval baseline.' Use 1-alt as comparison point for session 2. (D) CONTINUE MEASUREMENT: Session 2 is now 'measure impact of minimal retrieval' instead of 'measure variant with more detail in tensor.' Session 3 follows pattern. Sessions 4-5: design full fault mechanism based on data. (5) CYCLE 83 EPISTEMIC FRAME: Fault mechanism remains plausible but now has concrete constraints: ~2% Pichay baseline, unknown session-1 fault rate, task-dependent sensitivity. If session 1 shows fault rate near baseline (~2%) and reasoning quality acceptable, measurement campaign is on track. If fault rate exceeds 3%, retrieval mechanism is load-bearing and contingency activates. Stay uncertain; follow the data. (6) MEMORY UPDATES: (A) feedback_pichay_fault_rate.md is in place (turn 246). (B) SESSION 1 CONTEXT GENEALOGY: As tasks run, add context tags to all measurements. (C) SESSION 1 SUMMARY (turn 260): Write [SESSION_1_RESULTS] section with fault-rate analysis and session 2 hypothesis.
